q: Why is 'a container is a lightweight VM' wrong?
a: A container has no guest OS/kernel and no virtual hardware. It's just isolated, resource-limited processes running directly on the HOST kernel. A VM runs a full guest OS via a hypervisor.

q: Which two Linux kernel features make containers possible, and what does each do?
a: Namespaces (isolation — control what a process can SEE: PID, mount, network, UTS, IPC, user) and cgroups (limits — control what it can USE: CPU, memory, I/O).

q: What does the OCI standard give you?
a: Portability: an OCI image built by Docker runs under Podman, containerd, CRI-O, and Kubernetes. Standard image + runtime formats are why 'build once, run anywhere' holds.

q: What's the difference between an image and a container?
a: An image is a read-only template (a stack of layers + metadata) — the class. A container is a running instance of an image with a thin writable layer on top — the object. One image → many containers.

q: What is an image layer, and why are layers efficient?
a: A layer is a set of filesystem changes from one build instruction. Layers are immutable and content-addressed (hashed), so identical layers are deduplicated across images on disk and only new layers transfer over the network.

q: How does the Docker build cache decide what to rebuild?
a: It reuses a cached layer only if that instruction AND all instructions before it are unchanged. The first change invalidates that layer and EVERY layer after it — so order least- to most-frequently-changing.

q: Why copy package.json and install deps before copying source code?
a: So editing source doesn't bust the dependency-install cache layer. Deps change rarely, source often — ordering deps first keeps the slow install cached across code edits.

q: Tags vs. digests — which is immutable?
a: Digests (image@sha256:...) are immutable — always the exact same bytes. Tags (myapp:1.4.2, :latest) are mutable and can be re-pointed. Deploy by digest (or enforce immutable tags) for reproducibility; avoid :latest in prod.

q: RUN vs CMD vs ENTRYPOINT — when does each run?
a: RUN executes at BUILD time (creating a layer). CMD/ENTRYPOINT define the RUN-time command: ENTRYPOINT is the executable, CMD supplies default args (and CMD alone is the overridable default command).

q: Why prefer the exec (JSON-array) form of CMD/ENTRYPOINT?
a: The exec form runs your app as PID 1 directly, so it receives SIGTERM for graceful shutdown. The shell form wraps it in /bin/sh -c, which often swallows signals.

q: What is a multi-stage build and what does it achieve?
a: A build that uses one stage for compiling (with toolchains/dev deps) and a separate clean stage for running, copying only artifacts across. It keeps build tools out of the final image — often shrinking GB to tens of MB, with a smaller attack surface.

q: Why doesn't deleting a file in a later RUN reduce image size?
a: Each layer is immutable; a later layer can hide a file but the earlier layer still contains it. Clean up within the SAME RUN that created the file (e.g. rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* in the install RUN).

q: Why does a container stop 'by itself'?
a: A container runs exactly as long as its PID 1 process. When that main process exits or crashes, the container stops — which is why 'my container exits immediately' usually means the CMD finished or failed.

q: How do you read `-p 8080:80`?
a: host-port : container-port — host 8080 maps to container 80. Getting the order backwards is a common 'connection refused' bug. EXPOSE alone only documents; -p actually publishes.

q: Where should persistent container data live, and why not the writable layer?
a: In a named volume (Docker-managed) or external service. The container's writable layer is destroyed on `docker rm`, so treat containers as disposable ('cattle, not pets').

q: On a user-defined Docker network, how do containers reach each other?
a: By container name, via Docker's built-in DNS (e.g. api connects to db using hostname 'db'). The default bridge network lacks this, which is why you create your own network.

q: How does `docker stop` shut a container down?
a: It sends SIGTERM (graceful), waits ~10s, then SIGKILL. Your app should handle SIGTERM to drain connections and clean up — same signal model as the Linux topic.

q: What does a container registry do?
a: Stores and serves images as tagged repositories; it's the push/pull hub (Docker Hub, GHCR, ECR, Harbor) — analogous to a Git remote for images.

q: Decode the parts of ghcr.io/acme/payments-api:1.4.2.
a: ghcr.io = registry host; acme = namespace/org; payments-api = repository name; 1.4.2 = tag. (Or @sha256:... for an immutable digest instead of a tag.)

q: You build on arm64 but deploy to amd64 servers. What avoids 'exec format error'?
a: Build a multi-arch image (e.g. docker buildx --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64). A manifest list maps one tag to per-arch images so clients pull the right one.

q: What is the single most common container security mistake?
a: Running the container process as root. Almost no app needs it. Set a non-root USER in the Dockerfile and enforce --user at runtime; add --security-opt=no-new-privileges.

q: Why avoid `--privileged`, and what's the alternative?
a: It disables most isolation (all capabilities, full device access) — a major escalation risk. Instead drop ALL capabilities and add back only the specific one(s) needed (e.g. --cap-add=NET_BIND_SERVICE).

q: Why does a distroless image improve security beyond size?
a: It contains no shell or package manager and minimal libraries — fewer CVEs and nothing for an attacker to pivot with (you can't exec sh because there's no sh).

q: Why must you never bake secrets into an image?
a: Secrets COPY'd or ENV'd in persist in a layer forever and are visible to anyone who pulls the image — even if 'deleted' later. Inject at runtime or use RUN --mount=type=secret / a secrets manager.

q: Name three container supply-chain security practices.
a: Scan images for CVEs in CI (Trivy/Grype/scout); sign images with cosign and verify at deploy; generate an SBOM; pin trusted base images by digest and rebuild regularly to pick up patches.

q: There's no 'create container' syscall — so what actually starts a container?
a: The runtime calls clone()/unshare() with namespace flags, sets up cgroups, pivots the root filesystem, applies a seccomp filter, then exec's your binary. A container is a convention over a normal process, not a kernel object.

q: Name the Linux namespaces and what each isolates.
a: PID (process IDs), mount (filesystem mounts), network (interfaces/ports), UTS (hostname), IPC (shared memory), user (UID/GID mapping), cgroup (cgroup view). Namespaces control what a process can SEE; cgroups control what it can USE.

q: What three specs does the OCI define, and why do they matter?
a: image-spec (on-disk image format), runtime-spec (how to run a bundle; runc is the reference), distribution-spec (registry API). They decouple tools so an image built by Docker runs under Podman/containerd anywhere.

q: What does a user namespace protect against that a non-root USER directive doesn't?
a: A user namespace maps container UID 0 to an unprivileged host UID, so 'root in the container' is 'nobody on the host' — limiting damage from an escape. USER only sets the in-container user. Use both layers.

q: What is the typical container runtime stack on a node?
a: kubelet/Docker → containerd (via CRI) → runc → your process. containerd manages images and lifecycle; runc does the low-level clone()/namespace/cgroup setup.

q: When would you use gVisor or Kata instead of plain runc?
a: For hostile or multi-tenant workloads where namespace isolation isn't enough. gVisor runs a user-space kernel intercepting syscalls; Kata runs each container in a lightweight micro-VM — both add a much harder escape boundary at some performance cost.

q: What do seccomp and LSMs (AppArmor/SELinux) each do for a container?
a: seccomp is a syscall allow/deny filter shrinking the attack surface. AppArmor (path-based) and SELinux (label-based) are mandatory access controls confining what files/operations the container can touch even if compromised.

q: What problems do BuildKit cache mounts and secret mounts solve?
a: Cache mounts persist a dependency cache across builds (not baked into the image) for near-instant rebuilds. Secret mounts let a build use a credential without baking it into a layer — proper build-time secret handling.

q: Why use a distroless or scratch base image?
a: No shell, package manager, or extra binaries means a minimal attack surface and smaller image — fewer CVEs and nothing for an attacker to pivot with. Pair with an SBOM and image signing.
